narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the strengths in your background, like your upper-middle family income and choice to study full-time. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on profiles like yours, estimated a 52.9% chance that you would fail it—tipping just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This prediction was driven primarily by your academic rankings in school: your Year 3 decile of 1, which placed you in the lowest group compared to the typical student's middle-of-the-pack average, had the strongest pull toward forecasting failure. That factor outweighed your undergraduate GPA of 3.00, which is slightly below the average of 3.21 but still pushed noticeably in favor of success. Your Year 1 decile of 2 also contributed somewhat toward the failure prediction, though less intensely, while your LSAT score of 27—well below the dataset average of about 37—offered only a small nudge toward passing. In contrast, your family income bracket and full-time enrollment status both helped lean toward a positive outcome, aligning with patterns seen in many successful students. Overall, compared to typical applicants who pass the bar, your lower academic deciles and LSAT stood out as bigger hurdles than average, even with your solid GPA and background supports. This isn't a judgment on your potential—it's just how your profile stacked up against historical data. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score closer to the norm, strengthening your later-year grades through targeted academic support, and perhaps gathering letters highlighting your resilience as a Black woman in this field. Many students in similar situations have improved key metrics and succeeded on reapplication. You're capable of turning this around—keep pushing, and reach out to advisors for personalized strategies. We believe in your future.
